session_hint: the gRPC deadline exceeded error spike investigation
question: What was the root cause of the gRPC deadline exceeded errors?
turns: user

Root cause nailed in the first trace: our gRPC clients create a fresh context.Background() for each downstream call instead of propagating the incoming request context. Upstream callers impose a 5-second deadline. Downstream hops each start a new infinite deadline. The full call chain takes 7+ seconds but each hop thinks it has unlimited time. Upstream times out, retries, creates more load, all while downstream keeps processing the first attempt.
user
Checked serialization overhead as a possible cumulative contributor: proto marshalling across all hops measures under 1ms total. Not contributing to the 7-second total.
user
Looked at mTLS overhead for our internal gRPC mesh — handshakes complete in under 3ms and are cached. TLS is not the issue.
user
Suspected load balancer timeout misconfiguration: checked ALB idle timeout — set to 60 seconds, well above the 5-second upstream deadline. Ruled out.
user

Confirmed the deadline propagation gap by adding logging at each hop: incoming deadline shows '4.8s remaining' but the downstream call context logs 'no deadline'. The context is being discarded and replaced with context.Background() in the client factory.
user
This also explains why retries compound the problem — upstream retries after 5s, but the first request is still running downstream consuming resources. Under load this creates a retry storm.
user
Fix: thread the incoming ctx through all downstream gRPC calls using ctx.WithDeadline or just passing ctx directly. One-line change per service in the client factory.
user
Deployed to staging. Tested with a simulated 5s upstream deadline: cancellation now propagates through the full chain within 50ms of deadline expiry. No wasted downstream work.
user
p99 latency improved by 40% in staging even before load testing — fewer concurrent requests since upstream retries cancel correctly now.
user
Serialization, mTLS, and ALB timeout all ruled out. Deadline propagation was the only real issue. Adding a static analysis rule to flag context.Background() inside gRPC handler functions.
